data.title: AMD: The Wake-Up Call Is Coming
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) is in the middle of a structural transformation that the market still hasn't priced in. It's peeling away from its old image as a high-performance chipmaker and positioning itself as a vertically integrated infrastructure player for AI.

Despite AMD's 20% rally since my previous bullish call, the stock still remains significantly undervalued in relation to its structural earnings capabilities. The market still values AMD as a cyclical semiconductor seller, discounting its high-margin, full-stack AI infrastructure transformation and ramping free cash flow, as well as platform-driven value potential. A conservative sum-of-the-parts valuation, assuming realistic 2027 revenues and modest EV/sales multiples, places an equity value of $363 billion, or about $221/share.

Although legacy perceptions keep valuation multiples anchored to consumer cyclicality and CPU monopolies, AMD's real positioning is that of a re-architected business to take an outsized portion of the worldwide AI compute market. The market's inability to accept this shift presents a clear opportunity for multiple expansion, with forward estimates still out of alignment with the company's margin mix, strategic trajectory, and monetization scalability.

From Silicon to Systems: How AMD Is Quietly Rebuilding the AI Compute Stack

Central to AMD's transformation is its ascension from a product-led business to an end-to-end compute platform. The unification of CPUs, GPUs, networking, and software into deployable rack-scale systems is not defensive positioning against hyperscaler custom silicon, but an offensive reshaping of the value chain. This rebalancing is best seen in AMD's ramping growth in datacenter workloads. With hyperscaler deployments expanding beyond traditional computing into inference and frontier model training, AMD is becoming deeply embedded in the operational heart of next-gen infrastructure.

The intersection of EPYC CPUs and MI-series accelerators is generating not only incremental revenue but structural relevance. No longer is it about following Nvidia's (NVDA) benchmark in training throughput; it's about leading the TCO equation for inference and distributed AI. AMD's performance in scaling quickly from pilot deployments to production workloads, from commercial AI inference, recommendation engines, and sovereign compute clusters, all signal an advancing and quietly institutionalizing ecosystem. The relevance of this transition is highlighted by AMD's growing win rate with strategic AI accounts, such as several hyperscalers deploying its GPUs into high-memory bandwidth and low-latency-optimized large-scale clusters.

To complement this hardware improvement is the speedy maturation of AMD’s software stack. ROCm, which used to be an afterthought of the AI stack, is transforming into an authentic alternative to CUDA. Frequent container drops, growing Hugging Face support, and day-zero support for flagship LLMs are turning developer inertia into adoption. This indicates that AMD is no longer competing solely on spec sheets; it is competing on platform readiness. Just as global AI infrastructure spend is projected to grow more than 10X from $35 billion in 2023 to over $500 billion by 2033, positioning AMD to capture an outsized share of this exponential growth.

AMD’s Earnings Power Has Quietly Reached Escape Velocity

The stunning rise in AMD’s EPS and margin trend in Q1 2025 is not temporary, it is the company's structural transition to high-margin AI infrastructure at scale. Front and center in this inflection is the Data Center segment, whose mix, scale, and strategic alignment are reorienting AMD's overall earnings architecture. Operating income increased 57% year-over-year to $1.779 billion, and EPS increased 55% to $0.96, fueled by monetization leverage in EPYC CPUs and Instinct GPUs, broadening developer ecosystem lock-in, and disciplined execution with minimal dilution.

Data Center revenue increased 57% to $3.674 billion and now accounts for almost half of AMD's business. With a segment margin of 25%, up from 23% earlier this year, it contributed disproportionately to gross profit. This mix-shift factor, along with the reduced weight of embedded and semi-custom, lifted AMD's non-GAAP gross margin to 54%.

These dynamics are mirrored in the segment margin profile. Operating margins in data centers have risen above 25%, and client margins have increased from 10% to 17% due to average selling prices (ASPs) mix and operating leverage. Despite embedded segment softness and China regulatory headwinds, AMD is guiding full-year gross margin to normalize above 54%, indicating that both the breadth and depth of its profit engine are improving.

Without the $800 million charge for inventory related to new U.S. export controls, the company could maintain a consistent 54% non-GAAP gross margin, reflecting high-margin scalability and a shift in infrastructure compute mix.

EPYC CPUs continue to capture enterprise and hyperscaler market share with increased ASP, while the MI300-series AI GPUs, driven by ROCm software traction, have begun making a material contribution to top-line and mix. AMD’s positioning in inference workloads, memory-hungry LLMs, and sovereign AI deployments exposes it to large, fast-scaling use cases with higher pricing elasticity and longer deployment horizons.

Most importantly, it was all done with high cash discipline and without gimmickry. AMD generated $939 million in operating cash flow and $727 million in free cash flow (FCF), converting more than 90% of its operating income into cash. Even after spending $2.45 billion to acquire ZT Systems, AMD ended the period with $7.3 billion in cash.

Debt increased $2.4 billion to support the ZT acquisition, but the acquisition is strategically consistent: ZT brings rack-scale design expertise, enabling AMD to offer turnkey MI350 and MI400 AI systems that compete with Nvidia's DGX hegemony. The acquisition materially accelerates AMD's value share in the AI infrastructure stack and aids with higher ASPs and stickier deals.

Stock-based compensation, or SBC, cost was $364 million, a low figure compared to the $1.6 billion in net income. Dilution is limited to approximately 6 million shares, which is neutralized mostly by $749 million in buybacks. The net effect is genuine, high-quality EPS growth.

In reality, AMD's margin expansion is the result of strategic execution, not cycle timing. This is indicative of business evolving from a component provider to a platform provider, with ROCm and ZT Systems enabling full-stack deployment. Wall Street models continue to value AMD through an outdated, PC-biased, margin-capped lens.

However, the numbers tell a different story: AMD is building a structurally higher-margin, platform-anchored business that can support sustained re-rating as AI demand continues to compound through 2026.

Valuation Dislocation: The Hidden Valuation Flywheel Driving AMD's AI Re-rate

The present valuation conceals a structural shift that Wall Street still underprices. With a forward P/E of 25.6x and an EV/Sales ratio of 5.8x, AMD appears premium compared to the sector median. These headline multiples, however, do not take into account the company's growing earnings power and segment-level monetization leverage. Analysts estimate that in 2025, EPS will reach $4.02, representing a 21.4% year-over-year increase, followed by a 43% increase to $5.75 in 2026, with a forward PEG ratio of less than 0.9x.

AMD's forward valuation continues to lag behind its earnings growth path. On consensus 2025 and 2026 EPS of $4.02 and $5.75, respectively, the stock is trading at just 25x and 17.7x forward earnings, respectively, corresponding to an implied PEG ratio of approximately 0.55x even in the face of accelerating growth from AI. As AMD layers high-margin, platform-driven income from EPYC CPUs and Instinct GPUs, Wall Street's historical multiples do not catch up with the structural transition. Even modest re-rating to 22–28x on 2026 EPS merits a $126–$161 range, reflecting 26-61% upside as free cash flow and AI infrastructure leverage ramp.

This dislocation finds support in AMD's revenue growth projections, which anticipate a 22.4% year-over-year increase to $31.57 billion in 2025, followed by an additional 18.5% increase in 2026. Management confirmed that AI would remain the growth driver for 2025, led by MI300 accelerators, increasing hyperscaler adoption, and ZT Systems integration.

Why $221 Is a Realistic Bull Case for AMD by 2027 under SOTP

The discounted valuation of AMD for 2027 presents an intriguing case for meaningful upside from here, based on its transition from component provider to an AI infrastructure platform. By forecasting segment-level sales through 2027 and applying conservative EV/sales multiples, we estimate a forward EV of approximately $360 billion.

The anchor for the Company remains the Data Center, which has been expanding at a ~40% CAGR since 2021. The segment is set to reach $30 billion in FY2027 revenue, driven by the growth of EPYC CPUs, MI350/MI400 AI accelerators, and the adoption of ROCm. On a 10x multiple, reasonable, considering Nvidia's AI infrastructure comps, this segment alone justifies a $300 billion valuation.

Segment 2027E Revenue ($) Valuation Multiple (EV/Sales) Enterprise Value ($)
Data Center 30 10 300
Client & Gaming 12 4 48
Embedded 4 3 12
Total 360

Client and Gaming, fueled by growth through Ryzen AI and broad commercial notebook penetration, adds an estimated $48 billion of enterprise value (4x to $12 billion of revenue), and embedded, representing a high-margin but slower-growth segment, adds an additional $12 billion (3x to $4 billion of revenue). Together, these businesses have an EV of $360 billion.

When AMD’s net cash position of about $3.14 billion is included, we get an enterprise value of roughly $363.1 billion. With 1.64 billion shares outstanding, this equates to an approximate 2027 valuation of $221/share. This does not include additional upside from ROCm software ecosystem benefits or MI400-led rack-scale installations.

Lastly, the consistent multiple compression in both price-to-cash flow and EV/EBITDA multiples, despite enhanced monetization quality, indicates institutional reluctance to underwrite AMD’s transformation from a component player into a total-stack AI enabler. That gap represents the high-conviction re-rating potential when the company’s architecture and earnings align to produce stable, margin-rich growth.

Risks and Catalysts: Execution, Regulation, and Rack-Scale Ramp Sensitivities

Rerating is not risk-free. Execution of MI350 production and optimization of software is mission-critical. Inventory build reflects optimism, but also creates working capital sensitivity in the event of slippage in deployment timelines. The integration of ZT Systems, which is at the heart of AMD’s rack-scale plans, needs to be operationalized without diluting margins or introducing deployment friction. The opacity of the regulatory regime, particularly with changing AI diffusion regulations, may impede the scaling of global inference, especially in strategically critical areas.

Additionally, the MI400 pipeline is already being shaped with direct input from hyperscaler partners, indicating that AMD is being incorporated early into procurement plans for 2026. Demand for commercial inference is growing faster than expected, and AMD’s positioning for memory capacity and open-source compatibility is appealing to cost-conscious buyers scaling multimodal workloads.

ROCm, while still far from dominating, broke the credibility threshold. Each significant model launch backed at launch day lowers switching costs, and each optimization done natively in ROCm locks in an additional layer of developer trust. With ROCm nearing functional parity with CUDA in critical training and inference workloads, AMD starts to dictate its own ecosystem narrative, not just play in Nvidia's.

Takeaway

AMD is rapidly transforming into a vertically integrated AI infrastructure platform, yet the market still values it through a legacy lens. With $3 billion in annualized free cash flow, a $300 billion+ Data Center trajectory, and rising ROCm ecosystem adoption, AMD’s structural re-rating is not a matter of if, but when.

data.title: Dine Brands: A Wonderful Restaurant Operator Priced To Perfection
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Despite the cheap valuation and weakened earnings data, Dine Brands (DIN) appears to be a restaurant operator that is executing well on strategic initiatives and long-term objectives.

Declining revenues and operating income is mainly being caused by weakened consumer confidence and spending rather than a dissatisfaction with either the IHOP, Applebee's or Fuzzy's chains. This suggests that the current trough may only be temporary and that earnings will recover once consumers feel comfortable and are able to spend more of their income on eating out.

Considering that shares could be up to 70% undervalued, I think a real deep-value opportunity exists in DIN shares.

Strong buy rating issued.

Business Operations & Economic Moat

Dine Brands operates primarily as a full-service sit-down franchise restaurant businesses. International House of Pancakes IHOP, Applebee’s and Fuzzy’s Taco Shop form the backbone of their operations with these restaurants being present across the globe with the U.S. being their largest market.

To provide a little background, IHOP originally acquired Applebee’s in 2007 while the addition of Fuzzy’s was completed in 2022 which saw the formation of what we now know today as Dine Brands.

While this collection of restaurants may seem a little eclectic, they provide a solid presence in both the breakfast, quick-serve lunch and sit-down dinner eating spaces. Total restaurant count is currently over 3,550 with about 1,670 IHOPs, 1,560 Applebee’s and 116 Fuzzy’s.

About 95% of their outlets operate on franchise agreements. Much like McDonald’s (MCD), DB establishes a business relationship with a franchisee who in exchange for an initial part investment and an ongoing royalty fee and other fees receives the rights to operate a branded restaurant.

Franchising allows DB to grow the number of locations they have rapidly while keeping capital requirements lower than if they were to operate these restaurants as company-owned outlets. The franchise relationship is mutually beneficial whereby the franchisee is motivated to do great business as they themselves get to keep a large portion of earnings.

On occasion, Dine Brands will operate company-owned stores for limited periods of time, with the firm currently having about 50 of these locations. Often these locations have been identified as under performers, which the company takes back into their control in order to better assess what is going afoul.

While some of these locations get shut down they are also used to test new restaurant concepts or menu ideas.

When it comes to an economic moat, I am really looking to see evidence of a durable brand reputation, cost advantages or network effects creating pricing power for the company. It is only through pricing power that outsized returns and margins could consistently be earned from operations which itself would suggest an economic moat.

IHOP itself is a very popular and well recognized brand across the U.S., Canada and even abroad with their range of pancakes, breakfast foods and even lunch and dinner offerings being largely popular among customers. However, the chain faces fierce competition from quick-serve establishments like McDonald’s and Starbucks (SBUX) not to mention more direct rivals like Denny’s (DENN) or Waffle House.

Applebee’s Neighborhood Grill + Bar is a similarly well entrenched brand within the psyche of the North American consumer, although much like IHOP, it too faces competition from the likes of The Cheesecake Factory (CAKE) and Chili’s.

The chain has also suffered in recent years with a couple reimagining efforts failing to deliver meaningful gains. An attempt to move the brand into a more contemporary positioning ended up alienating their core older demographic of customers while also resulting in younger generations not appreciating the identity of the restaurant.

Since this misstep, Applebee’s has moved back towards a more traditional, family-friendly menu and restaurant theming which certainly better fits the expectations that having “neighbourhood” in the name of one’s restaurant invites.

Finally, Fuzzy’s is the smallest of the three Dine Brand chains with only 116 restaurants. The chain is a rather laid-back, fast-casual Tex-Mex joint that offers tacos and other Baja-inspired menu items to customers at relatively affordable prices.

I do not live near a Fuzzy’s, but after virtually visiting about 40 locations in the U.S. through Google Maps, the restaurants appear to resonate very well with customers with an average star rating of 4.3 out of five. Applebee's is about 3.9 while IHOP's is 3.8.

In order to assign weight to these three brands what I want to see the passing-on of cost pressures onto consumers as this ability itself would evidence pricing power. This ability does appear to be present with their gross and operating margin being very stable and having 10Y averages of about 46% and 21% respectively.

While same-restaurant sales have fallen over the past two years, Applebee’s and IHOP have had reasonably solid average growth of about 3% annualized over the past decade which is largely similar to the rate of food-away-from-home inflation of 2.9%.

This ability to maintain what appears to be a competitive position within the restaurant industry does suggest that their three brands are generating at least a narrow economic moat for the firm.

Regarding any potential cost advantages, Dine Brands is able to extract almost double the margins of rivals Denny’s, while having triple as much gross, operating and net profits relative to revenues as Chili’s parent firm, Brinker International (EAT).

While their overall cost structure relative to total sales is still about 20% higher than that of McDonald’s, I think their hugely lean operational profile is impressive considering that they are primarily a sit-in restaurant rather than a quick-serve establishment.

With these factors in mind, I think Dine Brand’s has a durable economic moat. A narrow rating is more appropriate in my view, as the relatively volatile sales figures combined with massive competition in the industry and a lack of any barriers to entry or switching costs for consumers limit the overall moatiness of any player.

Financial Analysis

Dine Brands is fundamentally a profitable and healthy enterprise. Pretty superb 10Y average gross, operating and net margins of 46%, 21% and 9% combined with robust returns on assets and total invested capital of 4% and 7% respectively suggest that the business is doing quite alright indeed.

A ROIC of 7% also exceeds the estimated WACC for DB which is currently around 4.4% suggesting that future growth in restaurant numbers will actually generate real value for shareholders.

Cash generation is also strong at the firm with FCF/share over the past five years outpacing EPS by about 10% annualised. I always like to see a company turning earnings into cash and believe Dine Brands excels in this regard.

The freshly released Q1 FY25 report has shed a little light into the current situation at the company, which, while not particularly brilliant, is perhaps not quite as negative as initially appears.

Revenues increased by 3.8% YoY in Q1 despite a 5% drop in franchise revenues as a massive boost in company restaurant sales from $274,000 to $21.6 million helped lift total sales.

This rather large increase was the result of DB acquiring 48 Applebee’s restaurants from franchisees. Declining franchise revenues were also caused by falling same-restaurant sales at both IHOP and Applebee’s. I suspect while the firm will try turnaround many of these Applebee’s locations, about 20 of them will probably be closed over the coming year.

Weakness in same-restaurant sales is pretty much exactly what I was expecting to see in Q1 results as the uncertain economic environment in the U.S. appears to be dampening consumer confidence. The consumer demand mix also shifted by about 20% across the restaurants towards more value-oriented offerings, which suggests cheaper menu items and new deals are working to attract customers into locations.

Applebee’s in particular saw increased sales and traffic volumes thanks to competitive promotion campaigns and an increase in off-premise (to-go orders, often placed through food delivery apps) business.

Similar efforts at IHOP helped increase traffic at stores. A really interesting little tidbit is that 80% of total food sales are breakfast items with business at dinner-time still consisting of 65% breakfast foods!

I think this illustrates how much consumers like the core product offerings at IHOP and confirms my earlier suspicions that simply focusing on producing the same great foods will work well for the chain in these uncertain times.

Echoing this sentiment is the rather outstanding success new dual-branded IHOP-Applebee’s restaurants are experiencing. Total store sales increased an average of three-times at test locations with multiple franchisees signing-up to convert more locations to dual-brand ones.

I really like this innovation and think the combination of IHOP and Applebee’s restaurants will help elevate sales across the country.

Total franchise expenses actually decreased 6% compared to the 5% drop in revenues. These efficiencies came thanks to continued efforts to reduce expenses for franchisees in order to ensure profitability for these partners.

Total company restaurant expenses of $22 million actually exceeded the $21.6 million in sales achieved by these wholly owned locations. I was expecting to see quite weak restaurant operating margins at these outlets due to most of these now company-owned sites being underperformers in need or reinvigoration or reimagination.

Gross profits for the entire company fell to $90 million or about 7% YoY implying a margin of 42%. I think this decrease is largely to be expected given the increased costs experienced through having the company owned restaurants which accounted for a significant chunk of revenue costs.

General and administrative expense control was at the forefront of management’s achievements in Q1 which helped cut these costs by about 2% YoY.

I liked CEO John Payton’s quick explanation of the costs associated with opening a new restaurant. He noted that the majority of equipment required comes from domestic U.S. suppliers, which should help mitigate against rising capital costs should tariffs rise further.

Net income figures fell significantly to just $8.2 million for Q1 due to the large $5.9 million impact closure and impairment charges had on earnings.

Despite all the challenges, I am actually quite happy with Dine Brands’ Q1 report. Great responses from customers regarding new menu offerings, combined with what appears to be a couple of understandable reasons responsible for the decline in earnings, increase my conviction that fundamentally, IHOP, Applebee’s and Fuzzy’s, remain in robust shape.

Seeking Alpha’s quant appears to agree with me as it supplies the restaurant a “B” letter grade. Even with these degraded earnings results, Dine Brands remains one of the most profitable restaurant companies in the industry.

DB’s balance sheet is also healthy. Long-term debt totals about $1 billion with maturities space evenly over the coming decade. Interest coverage is about 2x at present, which while lower than many other businesses, is still quite alright considering their degraded earnings profile and the traditionally low-margin business of running restaurants.

Goodwill also accounts for only about 12% of total assets which, I think, is reasonable considering their brands are what really drive sales at restaurants.

Cash and equivalents reserves of $187 million at present sit above the long-term average of about $140 million, which is wonderful to see given the uncertainty in present earnings.

Considering everything discussed I think Dine Brands is a pretty wonderful little restaurant business. Their lean operational profile creates resilience while a return to menu items that better meet consumer demands is helping bring customers into their locations.

While management does expect earnings for 2025 to still potentially be down 1-2% YoY, I think this has more to do with the general exhaustion of U.S. consumer pocketbooks rather than an inherent weakness at DB.

Valuation

The best place to start the discussion into DIN stock valuation is by taking a look at Seeking Alpha’s wonderful Valuation tab for the company. The current quant letter grade of “A+” certainly seems accurate, considering just how low some of these valuation ratios are.

A P/E GAAP TTM of 4.85x is very low, especially compared to that of Denny’s (9.2x, over double) or Brinker (18x, in other terms 4.5x DIN’s!).

A similar situation exists with their P/CF of almost 3x which is very cheap while their current P/S TTM of 0.38x only amplifies this sentiment.

These cheap key relative valuation metrics suggest that shares must be trading at heavily discounted levels relative both to the industry and DB’s long-term average.

Seeking Alpha’s 1Y advanced chart puts this data into perspective perfectly with the stock underperforming the S&P 500 (SPY) to the tune of 60%.

In the case of a mature and relatively stable business like Dine Brands, a DCF can be effectively used to understand what value may lie in shares.

With a DCF, I am trying to see what the present value is of all future cashflows from today until judgment day discounted appropriately against an opportunity cost and risk of investing in this individual equity instead of the market.

All of the FCF and balance sheet items came from Seeking Alpha’s very handy Financials tab for DIN. The average growth rate of 13% is skewed a little by the cyclicality of earnings, which is why I felt it is appropriate to reduce my 10Y projected growth rate to 3.5%. I think this is a conservative estimate for growth in FCF yet fair given the uncertainties that surround consumer spending, particularly in the U.S.

My terminal growth rate of 3% falls in line with inflation and long-term expected domestic GDP growth and thus implies DIN won’t become much larger a portion of the total economic output in the future. The discount rate is the 93-year annualized return produced by the S&P 500 and thus accounts for my aforementioned opportunity cost and risk.

With this data, I got an IV per share of $67 which, I think, is a reasonable estimate and implies a huge 70% undervaluation.

At the present time, this would imply a P/E ratio of 18.5x which is essentially the sector median. I think this gut check combined with the historic price DIN shares have reached helps increase the relative validity of my DCF calculation, even if the mathematics itself is correct in absolute terms.

While short-term (1-12 month) changes in share price are very hard to forecast given how volatile unnecessary food spending is, I think the long-term (1-10 year) outlook for DIN is much more positive given the cheap stock and still profitable enterprise.

Dine Brands Risk Profile

Dine Brands faces multiple risks including market cyclicality, massive competitive forces and executional challenges.

While all consumer discretionary companies are ultimately at the mercy of the prosperity of the general economy, restaurant operators are certainly even more exposed. As disposable income becomes constrained, eating-out and ordering food is one of the easiest and first things consumers tend to cut back on.

This reality is already present as can be seen by the earnings challenges DB is currently facing. While this may result in a temporary reduction in profitability, I think that the strong reputations of Applebee’s, IHOP and Fuzzy’s will help maintain long-term results.

Nevertheless, all three of DB’s chains face significant competition from rivals who are all vying for the same customers, be it at breakfast, lunch or dinner-time. The mid-2010s misstep regarding the management of the Applebee’s brand particularly highlights just how sensitive customers can be to poorly executed strategies and the difficulty of maintaining a steady stream of eaters in restaurants.

I think this lackluster period also leads me into my final risk for DB, which arises from failed execution. Any repeat of such poor brand management would most certainly compromise earnings and potentially could even do long-lasting damage to one of their eateries brand identities.

Current strategies focused on maintaining foot traffic through cheaper prices and better deals appears to be married to a “back to basics” approach regarding menu items which, I think, is working perfectly. Still, the risk of failed execution remains and the damage it could do is real.

With these three factors in mind, I feel the company and its stock warrants a high-risk rating. I urge all readers to complete their own research into these topics should they be of concern to you.

Summary

Dine Brands is one of the most profitable little restaurant companies presently in operation. Their healthy margins, returns on invested capital and assets combined with three durable eatery chains suggests that the firm is fundamentally operating successfully while at a wonderful level of efficiency too.

First-quarter 2025 results were certainly not all that positive, although I think the degraded earnings might take away from the volume increases and successes being enjoyed by new menu items and promotional campaigns.

The super cheap valuation also suggests that the stock may be slightly oversold and thus present a pretty compelling deep-value opportunity for patient, long-term investors.

With this in mind, I initiate a strong-buy rating for DIN stock and have personally started a position worth 20% of my entire portfolio in the stock.

data.title: Invesco EQV International Equity Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance quoted is past performance and cannot guarantee comparable future results; current performance may be lower or higher. Visit invesco.com for the most recent month-end performance. Performance figures reflect reinvested distributions and changes in net asset value (NAV). Investment return and principal value will vary so that you may have a gain or a loss when you sell shares. Returns less than one year are cumulative; all others are annualized. Index source: RIMES Technologies Corp. Had fees not been waived and/or expenses reimbursed in the past, returns would have been lower. Performance shown at NAV does not include the applicable front-end sales charge, which would have reduced the performance.

Class Y and R6 shares have no sales charge; therefore performance is at NAV. Class Y shares are available only to certain investors. Class R6 shares are closed to most investors. Please see the prospectus for more details.

Manager perspective and outlook

  • The first quarter of 2025 was marked by volatility and shifts in market leadership. Developed markets in Europe as well as emerging markets outperformed US equities. In Europe, German equities had robust gains, driven by increased deficit spending aimed at supporting economic growth and defense. Among emerging markets, China was one of the best performers as the country's stocks benefited from stimulus measures, including wage increases and expanded trade-in programs for consumer goods. Conversely, US equities declined, affected by apparent uncertainty about US trade policy and the rise of Chinese artificial intelligence company DeepSeek, which put downward pressure on US technology and consumer discretionary sectors. In this market environment, commodities, especially gold, had robust returns. Value stocks outperformed, while small-cap stocks lagged.
  • Our team remains focused on applying our long-term, bottom-up EQV investment process that seeks to identify attractively valued, high quality growth companies. We expect geopolitical and macro concerns to remain elevated, which has historically favored our traditional fundamental approach. We remain optimistic that our conservative quality growth style is well-positioned for the current environment.

During the quarter, we initiated positions in the following stocks:

Barclays (BCS) is a UK-based multinational bank with retail and commercial banking in the UK, a global investment bank and a specialist US consumer bank. We have seen improving visibility for earnings and quality after a challenging period. The company has been taking action to simplify the organization, cut costs, improve accountability and enhance capital allocation. We believe Barclays is attractively valued.

AstraZeneca (AZN) is a UK-based multinational pharmaceutical and biotech company focused on discovery and development of innovative medicines in areas including cardiovascular, oncology and respiratory. The company faced temporary setbacks due to employee investigations in China and issues with its oncology drug. Despite these challenges, we believe AstraZeneca's strong product pipeline and undervalued shares present a promising investment opportunity.

MediaTek (OTCPK:MDTTF), a Taiwan-based fabless semiconductor company, has been improving business quality through tech advancements, market share gains and winning new customers. Its new flagship smartphone chipset, featuring leading artificial intelligence capabilities, has been driving strong demand and market share gains in premium segments. MediaTek's application- specific integrated circuit business has also gained share, recently securing pivotal business with Google (not a fund holding). The company has partnered with NVIDIA (not a fund holding) across several business lines, and tech advancements have enabled price increases.

We also initiated positions in Australia-based maker of pooled pallets and plastic crates Brambles (OTCPK:BMBLF), China-based shopping platform Meituan (OTCPK:MPNGF), France-based electrical and digital building infrastructures company Legrand (OTCPK:LGRVF), Canada-based financial services company Royal Bank of Canada (RY), and India-based conglomerate Reliance Industries.

We sold Denmark-based health care company Coloplast (OTCPK:CLPBF) due to deteriorating earnings and quality trends. The stock's valuation had continued to trade at a premium relative to its sector, so we sold to make way for better EQV ideas. We sold UK-based pest-control company Rentokil Initial (RTO) due to questions about cost and timing for a turnaround of poor performance in its North American business.

We also sold UK-based DCC (OTCPK:DCCPF), Australia-based CSL (OTCQX:CSLLY) and China-based New Oriental Education & Technology (EDU).

Fund holdings in the energy sector outperformed those of the benchmark index, adding to relative performance. Having no exposure in real estate also added to relative results. Geographically, stock selection in India, Japan and Brazil contributed to relative results. Underweights in India and Japan were beneficial as well.

Stock selection in health care, information technology and industrials detracted the most from relative results. An overweight in IT also hampered relative return.

Geographically, stock selection in France, the US (categorized by country of risk) and China detracted from relative return. An underweight in China also negatively affected relative results.

Contributors to performance

Below are some of the largest contributors to absolute return for the quarter:

BAE Systems is a UK-based aerospace, defense and information security company that has benefited from rising global defense spending, given increasing NATO budgets. The company's record backlog provides in our view good visibility for earnings and cash flow growth.

Investor is a Swedish investment holding company that has delivered strong underlying performance in its unlisted portfolio and robust performance across publicly listed holdings.

TOTVS is a leading middle-market provider of enterprise resource planning software, based in Brazil. The company has exhibited strong top-line growth, with new ventures contributing more meaningfully, while its profit margin profile has been improving.

Detractors from performance

Below are the largest detractors from absolute return for the quarter:

Taiwan Semiconductor is a semiconductor and electronic components manufacturer. Apparent concerns about data center overexpansion associated with potentially weaker-than-expected AI growth led its share price to decline. We believe the longer term growth outlook for AI remains strong, with the company being the key supplier of semiconductors for AI data centers.

Broadcom is a leading designer of semiconductors that has benefited from strong growth in data center buildout to support AI applications. It has continued to diversify its customer base in the growing application-specific semiconductors business. The company's software business has also continued to exceed expectations. Broadcom's stock pulled back due to apparent concerns about near-term datacenter overexpansion.

Teva Pharmaceutical is a leading generics and pharmaceuticals company. The stock declined after a disappointing earnings update regarding profitability, which was affected by higher costs. Our thesis of improving earnings and quality as Teva's management focuses on building out its innovative pharma portfolio remains intact. Valuation relative to earnings potential remains attractive, in our view.

Invesco EQV International Equity Fund Q1 2025 by Invesco US

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: 2 Magnificent Dividends For Good And Bad Times
data.description:
Co-authored with Hidden Opportunities

Earnings season brings a surge of excitement accompanied by anxiety. Stocks make violent moves up or down in a matter of minutes, often before or after market hours. Options traders thrive on the chaos, while speculators either celebrate massive gains or lick their wounds after suffering painful losses. All it takes is one surprise: a miss on earnings estimates, a cautious forward outlook, or even unchanged guidance when markets expected an upgrade. When everything aligns perfectly, stocks soar. But even if one element is off, the punishment can be swift and brutal.

Investors tend to closely track every earnings call associated with their holdings, hanging on analyst commentary and obsessing over upgrades or downgrades. But here’s something worth pondering: have you ever noticed how analysts sometimes downgrade a stock after it already fallen +30%, or issue a downgrade while maintaining a price target well above the current price? These revisions often reflect sentiment more than fundamentals. The truth is, analyst opinions don’t affect the underlying business at all; they just stir the market’s emotional pot.

Market prices and emotional reactions to earnings are beyond any investor’s control. That’s why I choose to invest in ways where my returns are immune to speculation and more focused on the fundamentals behind that stock. While others are glued to screens, waiting to see if a stock will spike or crash, I grab some popcorn, settle in for a movie with my family, and let my portfolio quietly do its job. My strategy does not require babysitting, yet it produces reliable dividends that keep showing up and growing without my intervention.

Let us now look at two steady-eddies that simply keep growing their business and their dividends.

Pick #1: VZ – Yield 6.2%

Verizon Communications Inc. (VZ) is a stable business that was once CapEx heavy, but continues to reap the fruits of those investments in the form of steady FCF (Free Cash Flow) growth. At a +6% QDI yield, VZ continues to present an income investment at modest valuations, and defense against potential tariff impact on market and economic fundamentals.

VZ has had a busy quarter, with rapid progress on its 2025 objectives for service coverage and enhancement. The company remains on track to deploy C-Band to 80-90% of the planned sites by year-end, rolling out advanced 5G features, and beating their initial goal of 650,000 fiber optic passings for the year. In addition, management noted accelerated interest in its AI Connect offering, and the company is expanding its partner ecosystem in this area. During Q1, VZ also established satellite partnerships to enable free texting anywhere. Within fixed wireless access and fiber, the company continues to maintain its long-term goal of offering broadband to over 100 million premises, fiber passings of 35 to 40 million, and to have 8-9 million fixed wireless access subscribers by 2028.

These rapid enhancements were complemented by strong growth across key financial metrics. The telecom giant reported a 2.7% YoY increase in Wireless Service revenues and $33.4 billion in total operating revenues (up 1.5% YoY). The company maintained discipline with expenses, bringing its net income to $4.9 billion (up 5.5% YoY). Adj. EBITDA for Q1 was $12.6 billion, up 4% YoY, and Free Cash Flows came in at $3.6 billion (up 34% YoY), providing solid coverage for the company’s dividend.

VZ’s CEO, Hans Vestberg, noted that a very small portion of the targeted $18 billion capital expenditure for FY 2025 (midpoint of the guidance range) was exposed to any tariffs. It is noteworthy that VZ had no impact on its supply chain due to COVID-19, and expects the situation with the trade policy announcements to not affect their CapEx plans for the fiscal year.

Management reiterated their plan to close the Frontier Communications transaction in the first quarter of 2026 and that they are in the planning stage, with more updates to come closer to the closing. The $20 billion deal (comprising $10.4 billion of debt and $9.6 billion in cash), was approved by shareholders in November. The company notably lined up the debt in the form of short-term bank funding. VZ ended Q1 with net unsecured debt to adjusted EBITDA of 2.3x, and emphasized its capital allocation priorities to be to invest in the business, support and grow the dividend, pay down debt, and eventually get to a point where they can make share repurchases.

Management also affirmed the company’s 2025 guidance, expecting a 2-2.8% YoY increase in wireless service revenues, 2-3.5% YoY growth in adj. EBITDA, and $17.5-$18.5 billion FCF. Assuming an $11.4 billion spent on its annual dividend, we see a 63% FCF payout ratio at the midpoint. The Frontier acquisition is likely to keep the dividend raises small for the foreseeable future, and continue to expect a payment raise later this year in September, positioning Verizon for 19 years of annual dividend raises.

With its 6.2% current yield [QDI] and strong moat in an irreplaceable industry, VZ presents an attractive investment in an industry that is well-protected from the effects of tariffs and trade policy changes.

Pick #2: EPD – Yield 7%

Enterprise Products Partners (EPD) is one of the largest publicly traded partnerships and a leading provider of midstream energy services in North America. The MLP (Master Limited Partnership) owns a massive network of assets, and serves producers and consumers of natural gas, NGLs, crude oil, refined products, and petrochemicals.

Note: EPD is a partnership that issues a Schedule K-1 to investors.

The company reported its first quarter earnings on April 29th, setting a few operational records achieved during the quarter, in pipeline transportation volumes, marine terminal volumes, NGL fractionation volumes, and natural gas processing plant inlet volumes.

During Q1, EPD achieved DCF (distributable cash flow) of $2 billion (vs $1.9 billion last year), a 5% YoY increase. The partnership maintained a 1.7x coverage for its Q1 distribution, which was 3.9% higher compared to Q1 2024. After the distribution, the partnership retained $842 million of the DCF. We note that one of EPD’s Propane Dehydrogenation facilities was offline for 63 days during the quarter for unplanned maintenance. Management commented that the company’s DCF would have easily exceeded $2.5 billion had there been no downtime.

In the first quarter, the partnership repurchased ~1.8 million common units for $60 million, bringing the total purchases under the $2 billion buyback program to ~$1.2 billion. The partnership also made $1.1 billion in total capital investments in Q1, and maintains an unchanged guidance on the expected range of growth capital expenditures for 2025 ($4 to $4.5 billion) and 2026 ($2 to 2.5 billion). EPD reported $7.6 billion of major capital projects under construction, with $6 billion worth coming online in 2025. We expect these to be fully contracted under long-term agreements on arrival.

“I think we're pretty well contracted. I can't see us slowing down on what we've got under construction.” – A.J. Teague, Co-CEO

Tariffs were brought up many times during the conference call, but management reaffirmed their stance that they are very optimistic about their asset base and growth investments.

“Stating the obvious, a lot is going on that is causing nothing short of chaos around the world. Energy is not excluded. No one can tell how all the pieces land. So I think we must fall back on what we think we know.

The bottom line is the world needs U.S. oil, natural gas, and natural gas liquids to provide for their people and to grow their economies.” – A.J. Teague, Co-CEO

EPD’s debt portfolio is highly conservative, with an 18-year average maturity and 96% of the portfolio is fixed rate with a weighted-average interest rate of 4.7%. The MLP maintains among the lowest leverage levels in the midstream industry, ending Q1 with 3.1x, and boasted $3.6 billion in liquidity (available credit capacity + unrestricted cash) as of March 31, 2025.

Overall, EPD is well-positioned to maintain its DCF through trade uncertainties, and to reward shareholders with growing distributions. EPD stock is still ~10% below the levels prior to the tariff announcements, and this is an opportunity to lock in higher yields from this time-tested MLP with massive (~32%) insider ownership.

Conclusion

What kind of investor do you want to be? Are you going to be the type that refreshes their screens for earnings headlines, market reactions, and chatroom sentiment? Or do you desire a more passive approach without drama? I want my portfolio to pay me regardless of the earnings drama. Whether a company beat or missed estimates last quarter shouldn’t change the dividends I receive next month. This is why, at High Dividend Opportunities, we maintain a deeply diversified portfolio of +45 income-producing securities.

EPD and VZ are two companies that exemplify this approach. Their dependable and steady business model is capable of supporting growing dividends to help fund my lifestyle without the stress of market timing or surprises. Let others chase earnings season thrills. I’ll keep enjoying the calm, compounding power of income. That’s the beauty of the Income Method. It doesn’t rely on hype or hope – it runs on real cash flows.
